title: Simulation lane A. In /Users/masayuki/Dev/agent-orchestration, inspect scripts/local/check-kernel-task-completion-backup-agent.sh and tests/test-check-kernel-task-completion-backup-agent.sh. Falsify this proposal: add a freshness guard based on the log file mtime so stale normal-looking logs fail cl...
summary_text: ## Risks - The log-mtime guard alone is not a trustworthy liveness proof: a file can be refreshed without fresh `backup-scan` content (e.g., rotation, copy/touch/metadata rewrite), so stale semantics can still pass. - It adds a time-source dependency (`date`/`stat`) and introduces sensitivity to host clock skew, DST jumps, VM resume, or manual time correction; these can create false failures or false passes independent of backup health. - Hard failure on missing/odd mtime behavior can make the check fragile on macOS/BSD vs Linux `stat` formats unless dual-path handling is explicit. - A strict threshold can break during legitimate long gaps (agent paused, laptop sleep, launchctl backlog, transient scheduling delay), causing false alarms even when queues are healthy. - No guard currently protects against “good-looking stale” lines with old `pending`/`reconciled` values; mtime must be paired with current-line recency semantics (which the existing script already has partially via `tail` and `MIN_SCAN_PASS_LINES`). ## Edge cases - Log rotation where the current file is recreated: mtime becomes recent while old content is replaced with a small or empty file. - Manual log restoration/copy from backup: new mtime, old content. - Same-second timestamp granularity: very recent writes can produce age of 0 and bypass delayed stale windows if threshold is coarse. - Future mtime (clock jump forward/backward): age can go negative unless explicitly rejected. - Tail window (`LOG_TAIL_LINES`) being large/small relative to run interval can hide staleness if no fresh lines were written in that window. - Concurrent writes while check runs: race between `stat` and `tail` can produce inconsistent freshness/content views. - Read-only or ephemeral filesystems with unreliable metadata updates. ## Minimum safe implementation shape - Add explicit freshness options with a bounded policy: - `--max-log-age-seconds` (optional), default computed from `EXPECTED_INTERVAL` (e.g., `interval * 2 + 60`, capped with an upper floor). - Validate with positive integer check. - Compute age from file mtime in epoch seconds and fail closed (`EX_SOFTWARE`) if: - mtime unreadable, or - age > threshold, or - age < 0 (clock skew / future mtime). - Keep existing content checks: - existing `scan_pass_count` requirement, - existing queue-progress/lock checks, - existing `latest_log_pending` logic. - Add cross-check: - freshness guard should reference the newest `backup-scan` sample timestamp if present (if scan lines carry timestamps), else use bounded window + explicit `--max-log-age-seconds`. - Handle platform portability: - use `stat` with macOS and GNU-compatible fallback parsing before conversion. - Test additions (in addition to current suite): - stale log file mtime older than threshold must fail closed, - mtime fresh but stale-looking lines must still fail only via existing window/content logic, - future mtime and stat failure should fail closed with clear reason.
